Introduction
?

A square matrix is block diagonalizable if it is similar to a block diagonal matrix That is, there exists a block diagonal matrix and an invertible matrix such that

For example, the matrix

is block diagonalizable because it can be factored as follows:

Here, the matrix has eigenvalues

Notice that The eigenvectors of and respectively, are

Again, notice that The first column of corresponds to the eigenvector and the second and third columns correspond to the real and the imaginary parts of

The Block Diagonalization Theorem for a 3x3 Matrix
?

We have the following theorem regarding the block diagonalization of a real matrix:

If is a real matrix with one real eigenvalue with corresponding eigenvector and two complex eigenvalues with corresponding eigenvectors then where the matrices and are given by

In other words, if is a real matrix with one real eigenvalue and two complex eigenvalues, then is block diagonalizable.

Note the following:

  • The block of constructed from the real and imaginary parts of is a rotation-scaling matrix.

  • We can write the blocks of in either order. However, the order of the columns of must correspond to the order of the blocks.

Example: Finding the Eigenvalues and Corresponding Eigenvectors of a Matrix in Block Diagonal Form

EXPLANATION

Recall that if is a real matrix that has one real eigenvalue with corresponding eigenvector and two complex eigenvalues with corresponding eigenvectors then where Notice that swapping the blocks on the main diagonal of will result in switching the corresponding columns in

  • From our block diagonal matrix we deduce the complex eigenvalues are and the real eigenvalue is

  • From our matrix we deduce that the complex and real eigenvectors are

Question 1
?

P=[14−103−2203],B=[120−21000−2] Consider the nonsingular matrix P and the block diagonal matrix B shown above, where the 2×2 block of B is a rotation-scaling matrix. Given that A=PBP−1, what are the complex (not real) eigenvalues and the corresponding eigenvectors of A?

a
λ=−1±2i and v=[4±i3±2i]
b
λ=1±2i and v=[1±4i±3i2]
c
λ=1±2i and v=[4±i3i±2i]
d
λ=1±4i and v=[1±2i−2±3i3]
e
λ=−1±4i and v=[1±2i3±2i±3i]

Generalization of Block Diagonalization to NxN Matrices
?

We can find a block diagonalization for any real matrix This means that for any square matrix we can construct a block diagonal matrix and invertible matrix such that

where the blocks of are either:

  • blocks containing the real eigenvalues of or

  • blocks containing the rotation-scaling matrices corresponding to the complex (not real) eigenvalues of

The columns of form bases of the eigenspaces for the real eigenvectors, or come in the pairs for the non-real eigenvectors.

Let's see an example of block diagonalization for higher-dimensional matrices.

and

and

then where and Swapping the blocks on the main diagonal of will result in switching the corresponding columns in

From our block diagonal matrix we deduce that the first and second columns of will correspond to the complex eigenvector and the third and forth columns of will correspond to the complex eigenvector Therefore,
